does: This paper identifies and addresses the problem of exaggerated safety in large language models, where models incorrectly refuse to answer legitimate requests that pose no genuine harm. The authors benchmark this issue across multiple LLMs including Llama2, Gemma, Command R+, and Phi-3, finding that 26.1% of safe prompts are misclassified as dangerous. They develop and evaluate three prompting strategies—few-shot, interactive, and contextual prompting—to probe and adjust model decision boundaries. Through systematic testing with the XSTest dataset, they determine which approach works best for each model and demonstrate that combining these strategies reduces exaggerated safety refusals by 92.9% on average, enabling models to better distinguish between genuinely harmful and innocuous requests.
connects: This work builds on growing research examining safety alignment in LLMs and the tension between robustness and utility. It extends prior work on jailbreaking and prompt injection by framing the problem not as adversarial attack but as calibration—recognizing that overly conservative safety mechanisms harm model helpfulness. The paper engages with the XSTest framework and related efforts to systematically characterize model safety properties. It responds to earlier observations about false-positive safety decisions in aligned models, contributing to the broader literature on understanding and adjusting safety thresholds in instruction-tuned models.
enables: This work opens directions for developing more nuanced safety mechanisms that distinguish between harmful and harmless requests with greater precision. It suggests that model-specific prompting strategies could be integrated into deployment pipelines to reduce unnecessary refusals while maintaining actual safety guardrails. The findings point toward research on interpretability of safety mechanisms and automated calibration of safety thresholds during fine-tuning or inference. Future work could explore whether insights from these prompting strategies inform better training objectives that balance safety and utility from the start, or enable dynamic adjustment of safety parameters based on request type and context.
